name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ قَدْ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ لِبَاسًا يُوَٰرِى سَوْءَٰتِكُمْ وَرِيشًا ۖ وَلِبَاسُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (٢٦)
```

```english
‘O Children of Adam! We have certainly sent down to you garments to cover your nakedness, and for adornment. Yet the garment of Godwariness—that is the best.’ That is [one] of Allah’s signs, so that they may take admonition. (26)
```

```arabic
يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ لَا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ كَمَآ أَخْرَجَ أَبَوَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ يَنزِعُ عَنْهُمَا لِبَاسَهُمَا لِيُرِيَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتِهِمَآ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ يَرَىٰكُمْ هُوَ وَقَبِيلُهُۥ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا تَرَوْنَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٢٧)
```

```english
‘O Children of Adam! Do not let Satan tempt you, like he expelled your parents from paradise, stripping them of their garments to expose to them their nakedness. Indeed he sees you—he and his hosts—whence you do not see them. We have indeed made the devils friends of those who have no faith.’ (27)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا فَعَلُوا۟ فَـٰحِشَةً قَالُوا۟ وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهَآ ءَابَآءَنَا وَٱللَّهُ أَمَرَنَا بِهَا ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ ۖ أَتَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٨)
```

```english
When they commit an indecency, they say, ‘We found our fathers practising it, and Allah has enjoined it upon us.’ Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecencies. Do you attribute to Allah what you do not know?’ (28)
```

```arabic
قُلْ أَمَرَ رَبِّى بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ عِندَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ وَٱدْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ ۚ كَمَا بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ (٢٩)
```

```english
Say, ‘My Lord has enjoined justice,’ and [He has enjoined,] ‘Set your heart [on Him] at every occasion of prayer, and invoke Him, putting your exclusive faith in Him. Even as He brought you forth in the beginning, so will you return.’ (29)
```

```arabic
فَرِيقًا هَدَىٰ وَفَرِيقًا حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةُ ۗ إِنَّهُمُ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ (٣٠)
```

```english
A part [of mankind] He has guided and a part has deserved [to be consigned to] error, for they took devils for guardians instead of Allah, and supposed they were guided. (30)
```

```arabic
۞ يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ خُذُوا۟ زِينَتَكُمْ عِندَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ وَكُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ وَلَا تُسْرِفُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (٣١)
```

```english
O Children of Adam! Put on your adornment on every occasion of prayer, and eat and drink, but do not waste; indeed, He does not like the wasteful. (31)
```

```arabic
قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَخْرَجَ لِعِبَادِهِۦ وَٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ مِنَ ٱلرِّزْقِ ۚ قُلْ هِىَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا خَالِصَةً يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٢)
```

```english
Say, ‘Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?’ Say, ‘These are for the faithful in the life of this world, and exclusively for them on the Day of Resurrection.’ Thus do We elaborate the signs for a people who have knowledge. (32)
```

```arabic
قُلْ إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ رَبِّىَ ٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا وَمَا بَطَنَ وَٱلْإِثْمَ وَٱلْبَغْىَ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَأَن تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا وَأَن تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٣٣)
```

```english
Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies, the outward among them and the inward ones, and sin and undue aggression, and that you should ascribe to Allah partners for which He has not sent down any authority, and that you should attribute to Allah what you do not know.’ (33)
```

```arabic
وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ سَاعَةً ۖ وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ (٣٤)
```

```english
There is a [preordained] time for every nation: when their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it. (34)
```

```arabic
يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ إِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّنكُمْ يَقُصُّونَ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِى ۙ فَمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ وَأَصْلَحَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٣٥)
```

```english
O Children of Adam! If there come to you apostles from among yourselves, recounting to you My signs, then those who are Godwary and righteous will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (35)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ عَنْهَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٣٦)
```

```english
But those who deny Our signs and are disdainful of them, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]. (36)
```

## (Commentary)

Contemplating these addresses and the story of the prostration and the Garden that preceded them, and then comparing all of this with what has been narrated of the story and the address in other than this *surah*—especially the Meccan *Surah Taha*, which serves as a summary of this detailed *surah*, and the Medinan *Surah al-Baqarah*—guides us to the fact that these general addresses, introduced by His saying: "O Children of Adam," "O Children of Adam," are a generalization of the specific addresses directed toward Adam, just as the story was generalized in a certain manner in this *surah*, as we have previously indicated.

Of these four addresses introduced by His saying: "O Children of Adam," three pertain to warning against the sedition (الفتنة) — *the trial and temptation* — of Satan, and to eating, drinking, and clothing. These are a generalization of what is in His saying, the Exalted, in *Surah Taha*: **يَا آدَمُ إِنَّ هَٰذَا عَدُوٌّ لَّكَ وَلِزَوْجِكَ فَلَا يُخْرِجَنَّكُمَا مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ فَتَشْقَىٰ إِنَّ لَكَ أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيهَا وَلَا تَعْرَىٰ وَأَنَّكَ لَا تَظْمَأُ فِيهَا وَلَا تَضْحَىٰ** — "O Adam! Indeed this is an enemy to you and your mate. So do not let him expel you from paradise, or you will be miserable. Indeed you will neither go hungry in it nor go naked, and you will neither be thirsty in it nor suffer from the sun" (20:117-119). The fourth is a generalization of His saying therein: **فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى** — "Then if there comes to you guidance from Me..." (20:123).

It is known from the extraction of these addresses from his story and their generalization after specification, and then the derivation of other rulings upon which the aforementioned addresses were concluded, that these legislated rulings mentioned here in summary are rulings legislated in all divine religions (الشرائع الإلهية) without exception. It is also known that whatever has been decreed for man of felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة) and all other human destinies—just like the general rulings—all trace back to that story. It is the root from which these branches have sprouted, and the index that points to the details.

His saying, the Exalted: **يَا بَنِي آدَمَ قَدْ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ لِبَاسًا يُوَارِي سَوْآتِكُمْ وَرِيشًا** — "O Children of Adam! We have certainly sent down to you garments to cover your nakedness, and for adornment" (7:26). **Garment** (اللباس) refers to everything suitable for wearing and covering the body and other things; its root is a verbal noun (مصدر). **Feathers/Adornment** (الريش) refers to that which contains beauty, derived from the feathers of a bird due to the various types of beauty and decoration found therein; it is sometimes applied to household furniture and belongings.

The intended meaning of "sending down" (إنزال) the garments and adornment upon them is His creating them for them, as in His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَنزَلْنَا الْحَدِيدَ فِيهِ بَأْسٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَنَافِعُ** — "And We sent down iron, wherein is great might and uses" (57:25), and His saying: **وَأَنزَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ الْأَنْعَامِ ثَمَانِيَةَ أَزْوَاجٍ** — "and He sent down for you eight mates of the livestock" (39:6). Indeed, the Exalted has said: **وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ** — "There is not a thing but that its treasures are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (15:21). Thus, Allah sent down the garments and adornment through creation (الخلق) from the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — that is with Him to the world of visibility (عالم الشهادة), which is the act of creation.

The garment is that which man makes suitable for actual use, rather than the raw materials like cotton, wool, silk, or other things that man takes and adds industrial labor to—such as refining, spinning, weaving, cutting, and sewing—until it becomes a garment suitable for wearing. Thus, the garment and adornment are counted among the creation of Allah even though they are the work of man, similar to the relationship in His saying, the Exalted: **وَاللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ وَمَا تَعْمَلُونَ** — "And Allah has created you and whatever you make" (37:96).

From the perspective of the creative process (التكوين), there is no difference between attributing what man has made to Allah, the Glorified—since his work traces back to numerous causes, one of which is man—and attributing all that nature has produced, which also has many causes, one of which is the agent, such as the plants of the earth, the yellowness of gold, and the sweetness of honey. For all causes, with all the power they possess, trace back to Him, the Glorified, and He encompasses them.

Creation (الخلقة) is not attributed to things in a single uniform manner, even if all its instances agree in the sense of tracing back to Him, except for what contains the meaning of deficiency (النقص), ugliness (القبح), and heinousness (الشناعة) such as sins and the like. Their reality is the absence of good creation or the violation of the divine command; they are not created by Him, but are rather attributes of deficiency in the actions of man, for instance, in his inner or outer self. Reference to this reality has been repeated in previous parts of this book.

The description of the garment by His saying: "to cover your nakedness" (يُوَارِي سَوْآتِكُمْ) is to indicate that what is meant by the garment is that which fulfills the human need that compelled him to adopt clothing, which is the covering of his private parts (سواته) whose exposure distresses him. As for adornment (الريش), he adopts it for beauty beyond the basic need.

In the verse, there is a bestowal of favor (امتنان) by guiding man to garments and adornment, and it contains—as has been said—an indication of the permissibility of decorative clothing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلِبَاسُ التَّقْوَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ** — "and the garment of God-consciousness (التقوى) — *the inner state of godwariness and protection against sin* — that is best" (7:26) until the end of the verse. He, the Glorified, transitioned from mentioning the outward garment that covers the outward nakedness of man—by which he guards against the appearance of what distresses him to show—to the inward garment that covers the inward nakedness whose appearance distresses man, which are the vices of sins such as polytheism (الشرك) and others. This garment is the God-consciousness (التقوى) that Allah has commanded.

This is because the pain of distress and the humiliation of disgrace that strikes man from the exposure of his nakedness is of the same spiritual genus in both types of nakedness, except that the pain of the exposure of inward nakedness is more severe, more bitter, and more lasting. For the Accountant is Allah, and the consequence is an inseparable wretchedness, and a fire that reaches the hearts. Therefore, the garment of God-consciousness is better than the outward garment.

To point toward this meaning and complete the benefit, He followed the speech with His saying: **ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ آيَاتِ اللَّهِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ** — "That is one of Allah’s signs, so that they may take admonition" (7:26). Thus, the garment to which man was guided to fulfill his need for covering his nakedness—the exposure of which distresses him—is a divine sign (آية إلهية). If man contemplates it and gains insight through it, he will remember that he has inward nakedness that distresses him if it appears, which are the vices of the soul. Veiling them is more obligatory and necessary for him than veiling the outward nakedness with outward garments. The garment that veils them and fulfills man's necessary need is the garment of God-consciousness that Allah has commanded and explained through the tongues of His prophets.

Regarding the interpretation of "the garment of God-consciousness," there are other opinions narrated from the commentators. It has been said: it is faith (الإيمان) and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح). It has been said: it is a good outward demeanor. It has been said: it is modesty (الحياء). It has been said: it is the garment of asceticism and humility, like wearing wool and coarse fabric. It has been said: it is Islam (الإسلام). It has been said: it is the garment of war. It has been said: it is that which covers the private parts. It has been said: it is the fear of Allah. It has been said: it is what the God-conscious wear on the Day of Resurrection, which is better than the garments of this world. You can see that none of these opinions fits the context (السياق) with such precision.

His saying, the Exalted: **يَا بَنِي آدَمَ لَا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ كَمَا أَخْرَجَ أَبَوَيْكُمْ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ** — "O Children of Adam! Do not let Satan tempt you, like he expelled your parents from paradise" (7:27) until the end of the verse. Although the speech is separated from what preceded it by being introduced with the address "O Children of Adam," it is, in terms of meaning, a completion of the previous content. Therefore, He mentioned the nakedness (السوآت) again. Thus, the meaning returns to: You, O assembly of humans, have nakedness that nothing covers except the garment of God-consciousness with which We have clothed you according to the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — upon which We created you. So beware lest Satan tempt you and strip that from you, just as he stripped the garment of your parents in the Garden to show them their nakedness. For We have made the devils (الشياطين) allies (أولياء) for those who follow them and do not believe in Our signs.

From here it becomes clear that what Iblis did to them in the Garden—stripping their garment to show them their nakedness—was a parable for stripping the garment of God-consciousness from humans through sedition (الفتنة). Indeed, man remains in the paradise of felicity as long as he is not tempted by him; once he is tempted, Allah expels him from it.

His saying: **إِنَّهُ يَرَاكُمْ هُوَ وَقَبِيلُهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا تَرَوْنَهُمْ** — "Indeed he and his hosts see you from whence you do not see them" (7:27) is an emphasis of the prohibition and an explanation of the subtlety of his path and the hiddenness of his track—a subtlety that man's senses cannot distinguish and a hiddenness that his perception does not fall upon. For he sees only himself without realizing that behind him is one who commands evil and guides him to wretchedness.

His saying: **إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا الشَّيَاطِينَ أَوْلِيَاءَ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — "Indeed We have made the devils allies of those who do not have faith" (7:27) is another emphasis of the prohibition. Their guardianship (ولاية) and disposal over man is nothing but the guardianship of sedition and delusion. When he is tempted and deluded by them, they dispose of him as they wish and how they desire, as the Exalted said, addressing Iblis: **وَاسْتَفْزِزْ مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتَ مِنْهُمْ بِصَوْتِكَ وَأَجْلِبْ عَلَيْهِم بِخَيْلِكَ وَرَجِلِكَ وَشَارِكْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْوَالِ وَالْأَوْلَادِ وَعِدْهُمْ وَمَا يَعِدُهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ إِلَّا غُرُورًا إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ وَكَفَىٰ بِرَبِّكَ وَكِيلًا** — "And instigate whomever of them you can with your voice, and rally against them your cavalry and your infantry, and share with them in wealth and children, and promise them! But Satan promises them nothing but deceit. As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, and your Lord suffices as a guardian" (17:64-65). And He said: **إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ لَهُ سُلْطَانٌ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ** — "Indeed he has no authority over those who believe and put their trust in their Lord" (16:99). And He said: **إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ** — "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you" (15:42).

From these verses, when joined with the verse under discussion, it appears that they have no guardianship over the believers, even if a touch from them (طائف منهم) occasionally reaches them, and that he has no authority over those who trust in Allah among the believers—those whom Allah has counted as His servants by His saying: "My servants" (عِبَادِي). Thus, he has no guardianship except over those who do not believe.

It appears that what is meant is the lack of faith in the signs of Allah by denying them, which is more specific in one sense than the lack of faith in Allah, which is disbelief (الكفر) in Allah through polytheism or negation. This is because this disbelief is what is mentioned in the general address at the end of the story in *Surah al-Baqarah*, where the Exalted said: **قُلْنَا اهْبِطُوا مِنْهَا جَمِيعًا فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى** — "We said, ‘Get down from it, all of you! Then if there comes to you guidance from Me..."—until He said—**وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا أُولَٰئِكَ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ** — "And those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]" (2:38-39). And at the end of these verses in this *surah*, where He said: **وَالَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا وَاسْتَكْبَرُوا عَنْهَا أُولَٰئِكَ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ** — "As for those who deny Our signs and are disdainful of them, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]" (7:36).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذَا فَعَلُوا فَاحِشَةً قَالُوا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهَا آبَاءَنَا وَاللَّهُ أَمَرَنَا بِهَا** — "When they commit an indecency, they say, ‘We found our fathers practicing it, and Allah has enjoined it upon us’" (7:28) until the end of the verse. This is a return from the general address to the Children of Adam to a specific address to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in order to use it as a means to extract specific addresses directed to his nation, just as a similar shift (التفات) occurred in the previous address: "O Children of Adam! We have certainly sent down to you garments," where He said: "That is one of Allah’s signs, so that they may take admonition," for a similar purpose.

In short, from this established principle in the story of the Garden—which is the matter of the exposure of nakedness that led to the expulsion of Adam and his mate from the Garden—it has been extracted that Allah does not approve of heinous indecency (الفحشاء) among the acts of the Children of Adam. Thus, He mentioned the polytheists' committing of indecency and their attributing it to the practice of their fathers and to Allah's command. He then commanded His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to refute them by stating that Allah does not command indecency, and to remind them that this is saying about Allah that which they do not know and forging a lie against Him. How could it be otherwise, when the story of the Garden stands as a witness to it?

He mentioned two excuses they offer for their indecency and two foundations they rely upon: the practice of their fathers and Allah's command to them. The second was the one related only to the general address extracted from the story of the Garden; therefore, He addressed its refutation and rejection. As for their reliance on the practice of their fathers, although it is not something Allah approves of and He has rejected it in other parts of His speech with sayings like: **أَوَلَوْ كَانَ آبَاؤُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ شَيْئًا وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ** — "Even if their fathers did not understand anything and were not guided?" (2:170), He did not address its rejection here because it falls outside the purpose of the discourse.

A group of commentators has mentioned that His saying: "When they commit an indecency..." etc., refers to what was practiced by the people of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) regarding circumambulating the Sacred House (البيت الحرام) naked, saying: "We circumambulate as our mothers gave birth to us, and we do not circumambulate in the clothes in which we committed sins." It is narrated from al-Farra' that they used to make something from cut leather strips which they tied around their loins called *huf*, and if it was made of wool it was called *raht*. A woman would place a leather strap or something else over her private parts and say:

*Today some of it or all of it appears,*
*And what appears of it, I do not make lawful.*

This continued among them until the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) prohibited them after the Conquest when he sent Ali (عليه السلام) with the verses of *Bara'ah* to Mecca.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or some of the Muslims used to criticize them for that, and they would excuse themselves by saying: "We found our fathers practicing it, and Allah has enjoined it upon us." So Allah, the Glorified, refuted them and censured them with His saying.

What they mentioned is not far-fetched, and the verse provides some support for it as it described what they were doing as indecency (الفحشاء) — *a heinous matter of extreme ugliness* — and then mentioned that they used to excuse themselves by claiming Allah commanded them to do so. This necessitates that what they did was a heinous act they performed in the form of worship (العبادة) and ritual (النسك), such as circumambulating naked. The verse, despite that specific indecency, is suitable to apply to their act and to other instances that are so numerous among people, especially in the time we live in.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ أَمَرَ رَبِّي بِالْقِسْطِ وَأَقِيمُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ وَادْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ** — "Say, ‘My Lord has enjoined justice (القسط) — *the fair and equitable portion in all matters* — and set your faces [toward Him] at every mosque and call upon Him, putting your exclusive faith in Him’" (7:29). Since the previous verse denied that Allah, the Glorified, commands indecency and mentioned that such is a fabrication against Him and a saying without knowledge—as it does not trace back to any revelation Allah revealed—this verse hastened to mention what He has commanded. It is inevitably a command that opposes what the previous verse deemed heinous and counted as indecency due to its extreme ugliness, excess, or deficiency. So He said: "Say, ‘My Lord has enjoined justice...’" etc.

Justice (القسط), according to what al-Raghib mentioned, is the portion of fairness, like a half or equity. He said: **لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ بِالْقِسْطِ** — "that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds with justice" (10:4), and **وَأَقِيمُوا الْوَزْنَ بِالْقِسْطِ** — "and maintain the weights with justice" (55:9). *Qast* is to take the portion of another, which is injustice (جور), while *Iqsat* (الإقساط) is to give the portion of another, which is equity (إنصاف). Therefore, it is said: a man *qasata* if he acted unjustly, and *aqsata* if he acted justly. He said: **وَأَمَّا الْقَاسِطُونَ فَكَانُوا لِجَهَنَّمَ حَطَبًا** — "As for the deviant, they shall be fuel for hell" (72:15), and He said: **وَأَقْسِطُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُقْسِطِينَ** — "and be just; indeed Allah loves the just" (49:9). End of his words.

So the meaning is: Say, my Lord has commanded the fair portion and the adherence to the middle path of moderation in all matters, and that you avoid the two sides of excess (الإفراط) and deficiency (التفريط). So be just, turn in penitence, and establish yourselves at every place of worship where you worship Allah, and call upon Him by making the religion (الدين) — *the system of belief and practice* — exclusive to Him, without associating in His worship any idol or any of your fathers and leaders by imitating them. This is justice in worship.

His saying: "and set your faces at every mosque" (وَأَقِيمُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ) is apparently coordinated (معطوف) with the content of the command, because the meaning of "My Lord has enjoined justice" is "be just." Thus, the estimation is: be just and set (etc.). The **face** (الوجه) is that by which one turns toward a thing, and it represents the entirety of the human soul. Its "setting" (إقامتها) is the establishing of the matter for it, i.e., fulfilling it and performing it as it should be, complete and not deficient. Thus, the meaning of setting the face during worship results in being occupied with worship and cutting oneself off from other than it.

Therefore, His saying: "and set your faces at every mosque," when joined with His saying: "and call upon Him, putting your exclusive faith in Him," yields the obligation of devotion to worship apart from others, and to Allah, the Glorified, apart from others, as you have known. Among the "others" from which one must cut oneself off toward Allah, the Glorified, is the worship itself. For worship is a turning (توجه), not that which is turned toward. Turning toward it invalidates the meaning of it being worship and a turning toward Allah. Thus, the ritual performer (الناسك) must not remember in his ritual except his Lord and must forget all else.

Commentators have other opinions regarding the meaning of His saying: "and set your faces..." etc. Among them: that the meaning is: turn toward the *qiblah* of every mosque in prayer with uprightness. Among them: that the meaning is: turn during the times of prostration—which are the times of prayer—toward the direction Allah commanded you, which is the Kaaba. Among them: if you reach the time of prayer in a mosque, then pray and do not say "until I return to my own mosque." Among them: that the meaning is: intend the mosque at the time of every prayer in which the congregation (الجماعة) is commanded. Among them: that the meaning is: make your faces sincere for Allah through obedience, so do not associate an idol or anything else.

These views, despite their flaws and the verse's resistance to them, the first three do not suit the state of the Muslims at the time of the *surah's* revelation, as it is Meccan and the Kaaba was not the *qiblah* then, nor did the Muslims have various and multiple mosques. The last of the views, although close to what we presented, is deficient in explaining the sincerity (الإخلاص) derived from the verse; what it contains is only the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: "and call upon Him, putting your exclusive faith in Him," not His saying: "and set..." etc., as previously mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَمَا بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ فَرِيقًا هَدَىٰ وَفَرِيقًا حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الضَّلَالَةُ** — "As He originated you, so you will return: a party He has guided, and a party who deserved to be in error" (7:29-30). The apparent context is that His saying "a party He has guided, and a party who deserved to be in error" is a circumstantial clause (حال) from the subject of "you will return" (تَعُودُونَ), and it is the common aspect in which the return is likened to the beginning. The meaning is: you will return as two parties just as He began you as two parties, similar to His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونَا فُرَادَىٰ كَمَا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ** — "Certainly you have come to Us alone, just as We created you the first time" (6:94). The meaning is: you have come to Us alone just as We created you the first time alone.

This is the apparent meaning derived from the speech. As for "a party He has guided..." etc., being a circumstantial clause that does not exceed its operator, and the point of resemblance between the beginning and the return being something else not mentioned—such as their being alone at the beginning and the return, or the first and second creation both being from dust, or the resurrection being like the origination in the power of Allah, among other things they conjectured—these are views far from the indication of the verse. What benefit is there in omitting the point of resemblance from the mention and mentioning what is not needed while ambiguity occurs? The clarification of this will come, Allah willing.

The apparent meaning of the beginning in His saying: "He originated you" (بَدَأَكُمْ) is the first worldly creation of man, not the entirety of worldly life in contrast to the afterlife—such that the beginning would be the worldly life and the return would be the afterlife. In that case, the meaning would be: you were in the world created for Him as a party guided among you and error was deserved by another party, just as it traces back to the saying of one who said: "The meaning of the verse is: you will be resurrected upon what you died upon: the believer upon his faith, and the disbeliever upon his disbelief."

This is because the apparent meaning of the beginning, when attributed to something that has extension and continuity in a way, falls upon the earliest parts of its extended, continuous existence, not upon the whole. The address is to the people, so their beginning is the first creation of the human species and the beginning of its appearance. Furthermore, the verse is a completion of the verses in which Allah, the Glorified, explains the beginning of His creation of man with sayings like: **وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَاكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْنَاكُمْ ثُمَّ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ** — "Certainly We created you, then We formed you, then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam’" (7:11) etc. So what is meant is the manner of the beginning which He narrated at the start of His speech. It was part of the story that Allah said to Iblis when He stoned him: **اخْرُجْ مِنْهَا مَذْءُومًا مَّدْحُورًا لَّمَن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** — "Get out of it, blamed and banished! Whoever of them follows you, I will surely fill hell with you all" (7:18). In this is a decree (قضاء) that the Children of Adam will be divided into two parties: a party guided on the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), and a party truly astray. This is what He began them with, and likewise they shall return.

He has explained that in other places of His speech more clearly and explicitly, such as His saying: **قَالَ هَٰذَا صِرَاطٌ عَلَيَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ** — "He said, ‘This is a path [leading] to Me, [which is] straight. As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you’" (15:41-42). This is a definitive decree and a straight path: that people are two groups—a group over whom Iblis has no authority, and they are those whom Allah has guided; and a group who follow Iblis, the perverse, and they are those whose error is decreed due to their following Satan and their taking him as an ally. He said: **كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ مَن تَوَلَّاهُ فَأَنَّهُ يُضِلُّهُ** — "It has been written about him that whoever takes him for a friend, he will lead him astray" (22:4). Their error was only decreed following their adherence and alliance, not the reverse, as is apparent from the verse.

Similar to this is His saying, the Exalted: **قَالَ فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكَ وَمِمَّن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** — "He said, ‘This is the truth, and the truth I say: I will surely fill hell with you and with those of them who follow you, all together’" (38:84-85). For it indicates that there is a decree for their separation into two parties. This separation is what the Exalted derived His saying from when He said: **قَالَ اهْبِطَا مِنْهَا** — "He said, ‘Get down both of you from it...’" **فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ أَعْمَىٰ** — "Then if there comes to you guidance from Me, whoever follows My guidance will not go astray, nor be miserable. But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and We shall raise him blind on the Day of Resurrection" (20:123-124), which is the blindness of error.

After all this, it is possible that His saying: "As He originated you, so you will return..." etc., is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for the content of the previous speech. The meaning would be: be just in your deeds and be sincere to Allah, the Glorified, for Allah, the Glorified, when He began your creation, decreed among you that you would separate into two parties: a party He guides and a party who stray from the path. You will return to Him as He began you: a party guided and a party who deserved error by taking the devils as allies. So be just and be sincere so that you may be among those guided by Allah's guidance, not the astray under the guardianship of the devils.

Thus, the speech follows the course of His saying, the Exalted: **وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيهَا فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْرَاتِ أَيْنَ مَا تَكُونُوا يَأْتِ بِكُمُ اللَّهُ جَمِيعًا** — "Everyone has a direction to which he turns; so take the lead in all good works. Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you all together" (2:148). For while it first explained that everyone has a specific, inevitable direction to which he turns and from which he does not deviate—whether felicity or wretchedness—it secondly commanded them to race toward good deeds. The command would not be sound if one of the two stations—felicity or wretchedness—were inevitable. However, the speech is in the sense of our saying: each of you has no escape from a direction determined for him and necessary for him, either the Garden or the Fire; so race toward good deeds so that you may be among the people of the direction of felicity rather than others.

The matter is the same in what we are discussing. The speech is in the sense of our saying: you will return as two parties just as He began you as two parties by His decree; so be just in your deeds and be sincere to Allah, the Glorified, so that you may be of the party He guided rather than the party who deserved error.

It is also possible that His saying: "As He originated you..." etc., is a new sentence (كلام مستأنف), yet it is not devoid of an allusion to the call toward justice and sincerity, as is understood from the context.

As for His saying: **إِنَّهُمُ اتَّخَذُوا الشَّيَاطِينَ أَوْلِيَاءَ** — "Indeed they took the devils for their guardians" (7:30), it is the reason for the establishment of error and its necessity for them in His saying: "who deserved to be in error" (حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الضَّلَالَةُ). It is as if the word of error and loss was issued from the source of decree regarding them, conditioned upon the guardianship of Satan, as He mentions in His saying: **كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ مَن تَوَلَّاهُ فَأَنَّهُ يُضِلُّهُ** — "It has been written about him that whoever takes him for a friend, he will lead him astray" (22:4).

When they took the devils as allies in the world, error became deserved by them and adhered to them with a necessity from which there is no separation ever after. This is similar to what is derived from His saying: **وَقَيَّضْنَا لَهُمْ قُرَنَاءَ فَزَيَّنُوا لَهُم مَّا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ فِي أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنسِ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا خَاسِرِينَ** — "We assigned them companions who glosssed over for them what was before them and what was behind them. So the word became due against them along with the nations of jinn and humans that passed away before them. They were indeed losers" (41:25).

As for His saying: **وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ** — "and they suppose they are guided" (7:30), it is like an explanatory apposition (عطف تفسير) relative to the previous sentence, by which the meaning of the realization of error and its necessity is explained. For as long as man rides a path other than the path of truth and embraces falsehood while admitting it is falsehood and has not yet forgotten the truth, he is likely to return to the truth he departed from, and it was hoped he would be plucked from his error toward guidance. But if he believes in the truthfulness of the falsehood he is upon, and supposes he is upon guidance while he is in error, then the trait of perversity (الغي) has settled in him and error has become deserved by him, and no success is ever hoped for him.

So His saying: "and they suppose they are guided" is like an explanation for the realization of error because it is one of its necessary concomitants. The Exalted has said in another place: **قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُم بِالْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمَالًا الَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعًا** — "Say, ‘Shall we inform you who are the biggest losers in their works? Those whose endeavors go awry in the life of the world, while they suppose they are doing good’" (18:103-104). And the Exalted said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَوَاءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ خَتَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلَىٰ سَمْعِهِمْ وَعَلَىٰ أَبْصَارِهِمْ غِشَاوَةٌ** — "As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith. Allah has set a seal on their hearts and their hearing, and there is a blindfold on their sight" (2:6-7).

Indeed, man walks according to the primordial nature (الفطرة) and lives according to creation; he does not submit except to the truth, does not yield except to sincerity, and does not desire except what contains his good and felicity. However, if success (التوفيق) encompasses him and he is upon guidance, he applies what he seeks and intends to its true instance (مصداقه). He worships none but Allah, who is the Truth he seeks, and desires nothing but the permanent, eternal life, which is the felicity he intends. But if he strays from the path, his face is inverted from truth to falsehood, from good to evil, and from felicity to wretchedness. He then takes his desire as his god, worships Satan, yields to idols, clings to the earth, and becomes attached to worldly material ornaments. He looks toward them, but he only does what he does with the conviction that this is how he should act, and the supposition that he is guided in his action. Thus, he takes falsehood under the title of truth, and leans toward evil or wretchedness under the title of good and felicity. The primordial perception (الإدراك الفطري) is preserved for him, but he applies it in the station of action to other than its true instance. The Exalted said: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ آمِنُوا بِمَا نَزَّلْنَا مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن نَّطْمِسَ وُجُوهًا فَنَرُدَّهَا عَلَىٰ أَدْبَارِهَا** — "O you who were given the Book! Believe in what We have sent down confirming what is with you, before We blot out the faces and turn them backwards" (4:47). As for a human following falsehood as falsehood, and intending wretchedness and loss as wretchedness and loss, that is impossible. The Exalted said: **فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ** — "the nature (الفطرة) of Allah on which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation" (30:30).

None of the causes and reasons—including man—desires an end or performs an act unless it is suitable for itself, carrying what contains its benefit and felicity. What may appear to the contrary is only at first glance, not according to reality and the matter itself.

All of this is what contemplation and giving due consideration require of the meaning of His saying: "As He originated you, so you will return: a party He has guided, and a party who deserved to be in error" etc. It revolves around "a party He has guided..." etc., being a circumstantial clause explaining the point of resemblance and the common meaning between the beginning and the return, whether we take the speech as a new sentence or as occurring in the place of providing the reason, connected to what preceded it.

As for the majority of commentators, they seem to agree that His saying: "a party He has guided" is a circumstantial clause explaining the manner of the return only, not both the return and the beginning, and that the common meaning which is the point of likening the return to the beginning is another matter beyond it—except for those who interpreted the beginning as the worldly life and the first creation as previously mentioned and as will come. This was an attempt by them to escape the necessity of compulsion (الجبر) which invalidates choice (الاختيار) while the speech is surrounded by commands and prohibitions. You have known that this is not necessary.

In short, they differed in the point of connection of the speech to what preceded it after agreeing on that. Some said: it is a warning of the Resurrection to emphasize the previously mentioned rulings, and an argument for it through the beginning. So the meaning is: call upon Him with sincerity, for you will be resurrected and recompensed; and if that is far from your intellects, then take heed from the inception and know that just as He began you in the first creation, He will resurrect you and you will return in the second creation.

The flaw in this is that it is based on the point of likening the return to the beginning being their equality relative to the power of Allah, and that the subtle point in addressing that is the warning of recompense. The context suitable for this purpose would be to say: "As He began you, He will resurrect you and recompense you," by placing His resurrection in the place of the people's return and explicitly stating the recompense, which is the main purpose for which the speech was driven—as that speaker himself did in the meaning he mentioned—whereas the verse is devoid of that.

Some said: it is an argument against the deniers of the Resurrection, and its connection is to the saying of the Exalted several verses prior: **فِيهَا تَحْيَوْنَ وَفِيهَا تَمُوتُونَ وَمِنْهَا تُخْرَجُونَ** — "In it you shall live, and in it you shall die, and from it you shall be brought forth" (7:25). So His saying: "As He originated you, so you will return" means: your resurrection is not more difficult than your inception.

The flaw in this is the same as in the previous view, in addition to it being an arbitrary claim without evidence.

Some said: it is a new sentence. This has been mentioned previously.

Some said: it is connected to what preceded it, and the meaning is: be sincere to Allah in your life, for you will be resurrected upon what you died upon: the believer upon his faith, and the disbeliever upon his disbelief.

The flaw in this is that it is based on the intended meaning of the beginning being the entirety of worldly life in contrast to the afterlife, then likening it to the return—which is the afterlife—by the end of the first life called resurrection. The verse—as previously mentioned—is far from indicating this meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: **يَا بَنِي آدَمَ خُذُوا زِينَتَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ** — "O Children of Adam! Attend to your embellishments at every mosque" (7:31) until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: **Extravagance** (السرف) is exceeding the limit in every act man performs, although it is most famous in spending. End of quote.

Taking the adornment at every mosque is beautiful decoration when attending the mosque. This is naturally for prayer, circumambulation, and other remembrances of Allah. So the meaning returns to the command for beautiful decoration for prayer and its like, and its generality includes the prayers of the two festivals, the daily congregations, and all other forms of worship and remembrance.

His saying: **وَكُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا وَلَا تُسْرِفُوا** — "and eat and drink, but do not be wasteful" (7:31) etc., are two permissive commands and a prohibitive ban reasoned by His saying: **إِنَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ** — "Indeed He does not like the wasteful" (7:31). All of these are taken from the story of the Garden as previously alluded to. They are, as mentioned, general addresses not specific to one law over another, nor to one class of people over another.

From here, the invalidity of what some mentioned is known: that His saying: "O Children of Adam! Attend to your embellishments at every mosque..." etc., indicates the mission of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to all of humanity, and that the address includes women by following men legally (شرعاً), not linguistically (لغةً). Yes, the verse indicates that there are general rulings for all of humanity through one or more messages. As for the inclusion of women in the ruling, it is by way of predominance (التغليب) in the address, and the rational evidence for it stands.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبَادِهِ وَالطَّيِّبَاتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ** — "Say, ‘Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?’" (7:32).

This is the extraction of a specific ruling—for this nation—from the general ruling preceding it through a type of shift (التفات), similar to what preceded in His saying: "That is one of Allah’s signs, so that they may take admonition," and His saying: "When they commit an indecency..." the verse.

The interrogation is rhetorical/denunciatory (إنكاري). **Adornment** (الزين) is the opposite of blemish (الشين), which is that for which man is criticized. Thus, **adornment** (الزينة) is that by which the blemish is removed and the aversion of souls departs. "Bringing forth" (الإخراج) is a metaphor for manifestation and an imaginary borrowing (استعارة تخييلية), as if Allah, the Glorified—by His inspiration and His guiding man through the path of primordial nature to create types of adornment that he finds good collectively and which calls for the attraction of their souls to it and the departure of their aversion and disgust from it—brings forth for them the adornment which was hidden and concealed, and manifests it to their senses.

If man lived in the world alone without a society of his peers, he would never need adornment to decorate himself with, nor would he realize the necessity of creating it, because the criterion for realization is need. But since he cannot but live in a society of individuals who live by will, dislike, love, hatred, pleasure, and anger, they inevitably discover what they find good and what they find ugly of forms and fashions. So the Unseen Teacher inspires them from behind their primordial nature with what rectifies what is corrupted among them and beautifies what is blemished among them, which is adornment in its various types. Perhaps this is the subtle point in the specific expression by His saying: "for His servants" (لِعِبَادِهِ).

This thing called adornment is among the most important things upon which human society relies. it is among the deep-rooted etiquettes that accompany societies and progress or decline according to the advancement of civilization and culture. If it were assumed to be removed from its root in any society, the society would collapse and its parts would disintegrate from that moment, because the meaning of its invalidation is the removal of beauty, ugliness, love, hatred, will, and dislike from among them. There would be no instance of human society then; so understand that.

Then, the "good things of provision" (الطَّيِّبَاتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ)—and the "good" (الطيب) is that which is suitable to the nature—are the various types of what man is provided with by nourishing himself from it, or everything he draws upon in his life and survival, such as types of food, drink, marriage, housing, and the like. Allah, the Glorified, has equipped man with that which makes him feel his need for types of provision and calls for their consumption through types of desires raging in his interior toward what suits them of what fulfills his need. This is the "good" and the natural suitability.

The foundation of a happy human life upon the good things of provision is beyond the need for explanation. Man does not find felicity in his life from provision except in what suits the natures of his powers and tools with which he was equipped and helps him in the survival of his composition with which he was composed. He was not equipped with anything nor composed of any part except for a need he has for it. If he exceeds in something that suits his primordial nature toward what does not suit it naturally, he is forced to complete the deficiency occurring in the power connected to it by spending something of the other powers on it—like the glutton who overeats and is struck by digestive ailments. He is then forced to use medicines that rectify the digestive system and stimulate the stomach, and he continues to use them and exceed until he becomes accustomed to them and they no longer affect him. He then becomes an ill person whose illness distracts him from the general duties of life, the most important of which is healthy, free thought; and so on.

Exceeding the good provision changes man into something else: neither is he created for this world nor is this world created for him. What good is hoped for in a human who seeks to live in a circumstance other than his circumstance which the cosmos prepared for him, and walks a path that the primordial nature did not prepare for him, and attains an end other than his end—which is to expand by enjoying everything that desire and gluttony beautify for him, and imagination depicts for him to the last of what he can and the furthest of what is possible?

Allah, the Glorified, mentions in this verse that there is adornment He brought forth for His servants, manifested it, and explained it to them through the path of primordial inspiration. The primordial nature does not inspire except with something for which man's need has arisen according to it.

There is no evidence for the permissibility of an act among acts or the following of a path among paths stronger than the need for it according to existence and nature, which indicates that Allah, the Glorified, is the link between the needy human and what he needs through the powers and tools He deposited in his soul that drive him toward it according to creation and the creative process.

Then He mentions, by coordinating the "good things of provision" with "adornment" within the scope of the rhetorical interrogation, that there are types of provision that are good and suitable for the nature of man. He feels their goodness through his powers deposited in his existence, and he does not feel them nor realize them except for the establishment of his need in life for them and for disposing of them in a way he draws upon for his survival. There is no evidence for the permissibility of anything among acts stronger than the natural need and the creative poverty (الفقر التكويني) toward it, as you have heard.

Then He mentions, through the rhetorical interrogation, that the permissibility of Allah's adornment and the good things of provision is something that should not be doubted. Thus, it is from the Law's (الشرع) endorsement of the judgment of the intellect and the primordial decree.

The permissibility of adornment and the good things of provision does not, however, exceed the limit of moderation (الاعتدال) therein and the fair middle between excess and deficiency. For that is what the primordial nature decrees. Allah, the Glorified, said in the previous verse: "but do not be wasteful; indeed He does not like the wasteful," and He said before that: "Say, ‘My Lord has enjoined justice.’"

In the transgression toward either side of excess or deficiency, there is a threat to human society of decline and the corruption of the path of felicity, just as there is in the breach of a pillar among the pillars of a building a threat of its collapse. Rarely has corruption appeared on land and sea, or a conflict leading to wars that destroy progeny and ruin the inhabited world, except from the luxury of people and their extravagance in the matter of adornment or provision. When man exceeds the limit of moderation and transgresses what was drawn for him of the middle of the road, he goes his way without stopping at a limit or turning back for anything. Thus, it is appropriate that the whip of discipline not be lifted from him and that he be reminded even of the clearest things his intellect decrees. From this small amount is the divine command for the necessities of life like eating, drinking, clothing, housing, and taking adornment.

The author of *al-Manar* said in some of his words—and how excellent is what he said:—"One only knows it"—meaning the value of the command to take adornment despite its simplicity and clarity—"from reading the histories of nations and creeds, and knowing that most of the savages who live in thickets and forests as individuals and groups, taking shelter in caves and caverns, and the many pagan tribes in some islands of the seas and the mountains of Africa, all live with naked bodies, women and men. Islam did not reach a people among them except that it taught them to wear clothes by making covering and adornment a legal obligation."

"When some European Christian missionaries went to extremes in slandering Islam to alienate its people from it and turn them to their creed, and to incite Europe against them, some fair-minded people among them responded. He mentioned in his response that in the spread of Islam in Africa, there is a favor upon Europe by its spreading of civilization among its people by making them leave nakedness and obligating the wearing of clothes, which was a cause for the promotion of European textile trade among them."

"Rather, I say: some of the pagan nations with civilization, sciences, and arts were dominated by the life of nakedness until when some of them were guided by Islam, they began to dress and beautify themselves, then they began to manufacture clothes, and their pagan neighbors imitated them in some imitation."

"This is the land of India, despite the height of the civilization of the pagans therein in ancient and modern times; thousands upon thousands of their women and men are still naked, or half or quarter naked. You see some of their men in their centers of trade and industry between one naked who covers only the private parts—and they call them 'the two paths' (سبيلين), which is the Arabic word used by jurists in the chapter on the nullifiers of ablution—or covering only his lower half, and a woman with an exposed belly and thighs or the entire upper half of the body or part of it. Some of their fair-minded scholars have admitted that the Muslims are the ones who taught them to wear clothes and to eat in vessels. Most of their poor still place their food on tree leaves and eat from them, but they are better than many of the pagans in covering and adornment because the Muslims were their rulers. They were and still are among the highest Muslims of the earth in knowledge, action, and influence on the pagans of their land."

"As for the Muslims in the lands of the East where ignorance prevails, they are closer to paganism than to Islam in clothing and many religious acts. Among them are the women of the Muslims of 'Siam' who do not see any nakedness in themselves except the private parts, as has been explained before. Wherever Islam is strong, there is covering and adornment suitable for the dignity and progress of humans."

"Whoever knows the like of this knows the value of this reformative principle in Islam. Had this highest civil religion not made the taking of the adornment of clothing part of the law of Allah and obligated it upon His servants, it would not have moved many nations and peoples from heinous savagery to advanced civilization. Only he who is ignorant of history is ignorant of this merit of it, even if he is of its people. Rather, it is not far-fetched that there be found among the pedantic Westernized ones someone who sits in a playhouse, cafe, or tavern, leaning back and tilting his fez on his head, saying: 'What is the meaning of making the taking of the adornment of clothing a matter of religion? It is among the necessities of humans; they do not need divine revelation or religious law for it.' He might say the same about the saying of the Exalted: 'eat and drink.'" End of quote.

Among what suits the occasion is what was narrated: that al-Rashid had a skillful Christian physician. He said one day to Ali ibn al-Husayn ibn Waqid: "There is nothing of the science of medicine in your Book, and science is of two types: the science of religions and the science of bodies!" Ali said to him: "Allah has gathered all of medicine in half a verse—which is His saying: **كُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا وَلَا تُسْرِفُوا** — 'and eat and drink, but do not be wasteful.' And our Prophet gathered medicine in his saying: **المعدة بيت الداء ، والحمية رأس كل دواء ، وأعط كل بدن ما عودته** — 'The stomach is the house of illness, and diet is the head of every cure, and give every body what you have accustomed it to.'" The physician said: "Your Book and your Prophet left no medicine for Galen."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ هِيَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا خَالِصَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ** — "Say, ‘They are for those who have faith in the life of the world, and exclusively for them on the Day of Resurrection’" (7:32). There is no doubt that the address at the beginning of the verse is either specifically for the disbelievers or includes them and the believers together, just as they are all included in the previous verse's address by His saying: "O Children of Adam! Attend to your embellishments at every mosque and eat and drink, but do not be wasteful." Its necessity is that adornment and the good things of provision are placed on the basis of partnership among all people, their believers and their disbelievers.

So His saying: "Say, they are for those who have faith..." etc., is driven to explain the honor and merit with which Allah, the Glorified, has specifically distinguished the believers among His servants. Since they shared in His blessings in the world, they are exclusively for them in the Hereafter. The necessity of this is that His saying: "in the life of the world" (فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا) is connected to His saying: "have faith" (آمَنُوا), and His saying: "on the Day of Resurrection" (يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ) is connected to what His saying: "for those who have faith" is connected to—which is our saying "existing" or something close to it. "Exclusively" (خَالِصَةً) is a circumstantial clause (حال) from the feminine pronoun and was placed before His saying: "on the Day of Resurrection" to be a separator between His sayings: "in the life of the world" and "on the Day of Resurrection." The meaning is: Say, they are for the believers on the Day of Resurrection and they are exclusive to them; no one else shares them with them as they shared them in the world. So whoever believes in the world will possess its blessings on the Day of Resurrection.

By this explanation, what is in the saying of some becomes clear: that the intended meaning of "exclusivity" (الخلوص) is only the exclusivity from worries and disturbances. The meaning would be: they are in the life of the world for those who believe, not exclusive from worries, sorrows, and hardship, and they are exclusive on the Day of Resurrection from that.

This is because there is nothing in the context of the verse nor in the context of the verses preceding it that suggests the surrounding of worldly blessings with what disturbs the life of those enjoying them and embitters it for them, such that it would be a context for intending the meaning of exclusivity he mentioned.

Likewise, what is in the saying of another: that His saying: "in the life of the world" is connected to what His saying "for those who have faith" is connected to. The meaning would be: they are established for those who believe by original right and merit in the life of the world.

...the world, but others share in them by following them, even if they do not deserve them as they do. However, they are exclusively for them on the Day of Resurrection—or while being exclusive for them on the Day of Resurrection. *Nafi‘* read "خالِصَةٌ" (exclusive) in the nominative case (*raf‘*) as a predicate (*khabar*), while the rest read it in the accusative case (*nasb*) as a circumstantial qualifier (*hal*). This is because the believers are those to whom the beneficial sciences of a righteous life culminate, as well as the commands inciting the rectification of life by adopting adornment, seeking sustenance through wholesome things, and fulfilling the duties of livelihood, followed by reflection upon the signs in the horizons and within the souls, which leads to the creation of industries and arts used for progress in urbanity and civilization, and the recognition of their value and gratitude for them. All of that is through the path of revelation (الوحي) and prophethood (النبوة).

The flaw in this view is that if he intends that what he mentioned regarding primacy and subordination is the meaning of the verse, then it is clear that the verse is foreign to such an indication. If he intends that the verse signifies that worldly blessings are for the believers, and then explains the participation of the disbelievers with them as being through primacy and subordination, then you have already known that the verse only indicates the participation of both groups together in worldly blessings, not the exclusive right of the believers to them in this world. So where is the talk of primacy and subordination?

Rather, the apparent meaning of verses such as His saying: **وَلَوْ لا أَنْ يَكُونَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً لَجَعَلْنا لِمَنْ يَكْفُرُ بِالرَّحْمنِ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ سُقُفاً مِنْ فِضَّةٍ وَمَعارِجَ عَلَيْها يَظْهَرُونَ** — until He said — **وَإِنْ كُلُّ ذلِكَ لَمَّا مَتاعُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةُ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ** — "Were it not that mankind would have become a single community, We would have surely provided for those who disbelieve in the All-beneficent, silver roofs for their houses and [silver] stairs by which they ascend... All these are nothing but the wares of the life of this world, and the Hereafter with your Lord is for the God-conscious (المتقين)." (Az-Zukhruf: 33-35) — suggests the opposite: that the splendor of the life of this world is more fitting to be specialized for them [the disbelievers].

Allah, the Exalted, has bestowed His favor at the end of the verse upon the people of knowledge by detailing the explanation, as He said: **كَذلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ** — "Thus do We elaborate the signs for a people who have knowledge."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ** — "Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies (الفواحش), whether outward or inward...’" to the end of the verse. The exhaustive discussion regarding the vocabulary of the verse has preceded. **Indecencies (الفواحش)** are sins reaching the peak of ugliness and heinousness, such as adultery, sodomy, and the like. **Sin (الإثم)** is the transgression that results in the degradation of a human being in his life, leading to humiliation, abasement, and downfall, such as drinking wine, which results in the destruction of one's prestige, wealth, honor, and soul. **Aggression (البغي)** is a person seeking what is not his by right, such as types of injustice, transgression against people, and unlawful domination over them. Describing it as being "without right" is a description of a necessary attribute, similar to the restriction in His saying: **ما لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ سُلْطاناً** — "that for which He has not sent down any authority."

The address permitting adornment and wholesome sustenance invited the soul of the listener to obtain what Allah had forbidden. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, presented in this verse the sum total of the matter, and nothing of the religious prohibitions escapes what He mentioned. These are divided into two categories: those pertaining to actions—which are the first three—and those pertaining to speech and beliefs—which are the last two. Of the first category, some pertain to people, which is aggression without right, and others do not; the latter is either characterized by ugliness and heinousness, which is indecency (الفاحشة), or it is not, which is sin (الإثم). The second category is either polytheism (الشرك) against Allah or fabrication (الافتراء) against Allah, the Glorified.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ** — "There is a term for every nation..." to the end of the verse. This is a reality extracted from the saying of the Exalted at the end of the story: **قالَ فِيها تَحْيَوْنَ وَفِيها تَمُوتُونَ وَمِنْها تُخْرَجُونَ** — "He said, ‘In it you shall live, and in it you shall die, and from it you shall be brought forth.’" It is similar to the other rulings extracted from it mentioned previously. Its meaning is that nations and societies have lifespans and terms (آجال) similar to those of individuals.

Perhaps it can be inferred from this derivation and extraction that the saying of the Exalted at the end of the previous story: **قالَ فِيها تَحْيَوْنَ** — "He said, ‘In it you shall live...’" etc., refers to the life of every single individual and every single nation, which is a portion of the lifespan of general humanity. Meanwhile, His saying before it: **وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ** — "and there will be for you on the earth an abode and enjoyment for a while" refers to the life of the species until a time, which is the time of extinction or resurrection. This is the lifespan of general humanity in this world.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا بَنِي آدَمَ إِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِنْكُمْ يَقُصُّونَ عَلَيْكُمْ آياتِي** — "O Children of Adam! If there come to you messengers from among yourselves, relating to you My signs..." to the end of the two verses. **إِمَّا** is originally the conditional "if" (إن الشرطية) combined with the augmentative "ma" (ما), and its condition contains the emphatic "nun" (النون الثقيلة). This seems to signify that the condition will inevitably be realized. The meaning of "relating the signs" (قص الآيات) is their explanation and detailing, due to the sense of cutting and making manifest what was hidden.

The verse is one of the general addresses extracted from the story of the Garden mentioned here; it is the fourth and last of them. It explains to people the general divine legislation (التشريع) for religion through following the message and the path of revelation. The principle from which it is extracted is like His saying in Surah Ta-Ha: **قالَ اهْبِطا مِنْها جَمِيعاً بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ مِنِّي هُدىً** — "He said, ‘Get down from it, all together, being enemies of one another! Yet if there comes to you a guidance from Me...’" etc. Thus, He explained that the coming of guidance from Him occurs only through the path of the message (الرسالة).

## (Commentary)

In *ad-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from ‘Ikrimah regarding His saying: **قَدْ أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكُمْ لِباساً يُوارِي سَوْآتِكُمْ** — "We have sent down to you clothing to cover your nakedness." He said: "It was revealed concerning the *Khums* of Quraysh—and those who followed their way among the Arab tribes: the Ansar, al-Aws, al-Khazraj, Khuza‘ah, Thaqif, Banu ‘Amir ibn Sa‘sa‘ah, and the clans of Kinanah ibn Bakr. They would not eat meat, nor enter houses except from their back doors. They would not use tents made of camel hair or wool, but only tents made of leather. They would make their children wear loincloths (*al-rihat*), and they used to circumambulate [the Ka‘bah] naked, except for Quraysh. When they arrived, they would cast off the clothes they came in and say: 'These are our clothes in which we have purified ourselves—to our Lord—from sins and mistakes.' Then they would say to Quraysh: 'Who will lend us a waist-wrap?' If they found none, they circumambulated naked. When they finished their circumambulation, they took the clothes they had set aside."

In the same source, ‘Abd ibn Humayd narrated from Sa‘id ibn Jubayr, who said: "People used to circumambulate the House naked, saying: 'We will not circumambulate in clothes in which we have sinned.' A woman came, cast off her clothes, and circumambulated, placing her hand over her private parts and saying:

*Today some of it or all of it appears,*
*And what appears of it, I do not make lawful.*

Then this verse was revealed: **خُذُوا زِينَتَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ** — 'Put on your adornment at every mosque...' until His saying: **وَالطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ** — 'and the good things of [His] provision.'"

**I say:** Something similar has been narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, Mujahid, and ‘Ata’. However, you have already known that the verses beginning with His saying **يا بَنِي آدَمَ** (O Children of Adam) are general rulings and legislations for all the children of Adam without being specific to one nation over another. Thus, these individual reports are no more than the personal judgment (*ijtihad*) of those from whom they are narrated and possess no binding authority (*hujjiyyah*). The most balanced narrations in this regard are the two following reports.

In *ad-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Men used to circumambulate the House naked, so Allah commanded them to wear adornment (الزينة), and adornment is clothing—which is what covers the nakedness—and other than that of fine garments and goods."

In the same source: Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "The people of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) used to forbid things that Allah had made lawful—of clothing and other things—and that is the saying of Allah: **قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ لَكُمْ مِنْ رِزْقٍ فَجَعَلْتُمْ مِنْهُ حَراماً وَحَلالاً** — 'Say, "Tell me, what has Allah sent down to you of provision, and then you made some of it unlawful and [some] lawful?"' (Yunus: 59). It is this. So Allah revealed: **قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبادِهِ وَالطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ قُلْ هِيَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** — 'Say, "Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?" Say, "These are for the faithful in the life of this world..."' (Al-A‘raf: 32). Meaning: the Muslims shared with the disbelievers in the good things in the life of this world—so they ate of its good food, wore its fine clothes, and married its righteous women—then Allah will make the good things exclusive in the Hereafter for those who believe, and the polytheists will have nothing of them."

**I say:** The two narrations—as you see—are apparent in being an application (تطبيق) of the verse rather than the specific occasion of revelation (سبب النزول), and that is what is to be relied upon.

In the same source: Abu al-Shaykh narrated from al-Hasan, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'There is no servant who performs a good or evil deed except that he is clothed in the cloak of his deed until they recognize him.' The confirmation of that is in the Book of Allah: **وَلِباسُ التَّقْوى ذلِكَ خَيْرٌ** — 'and the garment of God-consciousness (التقوى)—that is best.'"

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام: regarding His saying: **يا بَنِي آدَمَ قَدْ أَنْزَلْنا** — "O Children of Adam! We have certainly sent down..." the verse. "The garment of God-consciousness (لباس التقوى) is white clothes."

In *ad-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from ‘Uthman: "I heard the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله reciting: 'وَرِياشاً' (*riyashan*) and he did not say: 'وَرِيشاً' (*rishan*)."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, it is said: In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام: regarding the saying of the Exalted: **يا بَنِي آدَمَ قَدْ أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكُمْ لِباساً يُوارِي سَوْآتِكُمْ وَرِيشاً وَلِباسُ التَّقْوى** — "O Children of Adam! We have certainly sent down to you clothing to cover your nakedness, and plumage (ريشا), and the garment of God-consciousness..." He said: "As for the clothing (اللباس), it is the clothes you wear. As for the plumage (الرياش), it is goods and wealth. As for the garment of God-consciousness (لباس التقوى), it is chastity (العفاف); for the chaste person, his nakedness does not appear even if he is bare of clothing, while the profligate (الفاجر) has his nakedness exposed even if he is clothed in garments."

**I say:** What is in the two narrations regarding the meaning of the garment of God-consciousness is an instance of taking some of the specific applications (المصاديق), and similar instances have been repeated in the narrations.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, also regarding the saying of the Exalted: **وَإِذا فَعَلُوا فاحِشَةً قالُوا** — "When they commit an indecency, they say..." the verse. He said: "Those who worshipped idols said [this], so Allah refuted them and said: **قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشاءِ** — 'Say, "Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecency..."' to the end of the verse."

In *al-Basa'ir*, from Ahmad ibn Muhammad, from al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id, from Muhammad ibn Mansur, who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: **وَإِذا فَعَلُوا فاحِشَةً قالُوا وَجَدْنا عَلَيْها آباءَنا وَاللهُ أَمَرَنا بِها** — 'When they commit an indecency, they say, "We found our fathers practicing it, and Allah has enjoined it upon us..."' to the end of the verse. He said: 'Have you seen anyone claim that Allah commanded us to commit adultery, drink wine, or any of the prohibitions?' I said: 'No.' He said: 'Then what is this indecency (الفاحشة) that they claim Allah commanded us with?' I said: 'Allah and His Messenger know best.' He said: 'This refers to the leaders of injustice (أئمة الجور); they claimed that Allah commanded following a people whom Allah did not command [to be followed]. So Allah refuted them and informed us that they spoke a lie against Him—and Allah called that an indecency (فاحشة) on their part.'"

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Kafi* from several of our companions from Ahmad ibn Muhammad from al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id from Abu Wahb from Muhammad ibn Mansur, who said: "I asked him..." and he cited the hadith. Something similar was narrated in *Tafsir al-Ayyashi* from Muhammad ibn Mansur from a righteous servant (عبد صالح). Thus, it is known that the chain of transmission includes Abu Wahb, and from him al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id narrates, and that the hadith is narrated from Musa ibn Ja‘far عليه‌السلام.

In any case, the narration does not correspond in its content to the time of the verse's revelation, nor does the argument mentioned in it correspond to its specific context. For the people of the Age of Ignorance had many rulings related to matters of the nature of indecency which they attributed to Allah, the Glorified, such as circumambulating the House naked.

However, the argument mentioned in it, in terms of the verse's application to instances after the time of revelation, is more closely applicable to the leaders of injustice and tyrannical rulers. For the Muslims passed through eras in which their affairs were governed by the likes of the pretender Ziyad ibn Abih, his son ‘Ubaydullah, al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, and other tyrants. Around their thrones and seats were a number of scholars issuing fatwas on the validity of their rulings and the obligation of obeying them using verses such as the saying of the Exalted: **أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ** — "Obey Allah and obey the Messenger and those vested with authority among you." (An-Nisa: 59). Thus, the narration is looking at the application of the verse to its instances after the era of revelation.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I heard him saying: 'Whoever claims that Allah commands indecency (الفاحشة) has lied against Allah, and whoever claims that good and evil are [solely] up to Him has lied against Allah.'"

In the same source: from Mas‘adah ibn Sadaqah from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام: "Whoever claims that Allah commanded evil and indecency has lied against Allah. Whoever claims that good and evil occur without His will (مشية) has removed Allah from His sovereignty. Whoever claims that sins are committed without the power (قوة) of Allah has lied against Allah, and whoever lies against Allah, Allah will enter him into the Fire."

**I say:** His saying عليه‌السلام: "And whoever claims that good and evil occur without His will..." etc., refers to the view of the *Mufawwidah* (the delegators) regarding the independence of the servant in acts of good and evil. Similarly, his saying in the previous narration: "And whoever claims that good and evil are up to Him..." etc., refers to the view of the *Mujabbirah* (the predestinarians): that good, evil, obedience, and disobedience are attributed only to the will of Allah without the will and choice of the servant having any role in the occurrence of the act—though it is possible in a way to refer the pronoun to the servant so that it points to the view of the *Mufawwidah*.

In *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Muskan from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah عز وجل: **وَأَقِيمُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ** — 'and set your faces upright at every mosque.' He said: 'This is the *Qiblah* (القبلة).'"

**I say:** This is of the nature of "flow and application" (الجري والانطباق) as became clear from the previous commentary. Al-Ayyashi narrated the same in his *Tafsir* from Abu Basir from one of the two [Imams] عليهما‌السلام.

In *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain of transmission from al-Halabi from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, and in *Tafsir al-Ayyashi* from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: **وَأَقِيمُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ**: "Newly established mosques—so they were commanded to set their faces toward the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام)."

**I say:** It is apparent that his meaning عليه‌السلام is that the sense of "setting the faces" in the verse is turning toward Allah by facing the *Qiblah* at every mosque where prayer is performed. Then the *Qiblah* was specified by verses such as: **فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ وَحَيْثُ ما كُنْتُمْ فَوَلُّوا وُجُوهَكُمْ شَطْرَهُ** — "so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque, and wherever you may be, turn your faces towards it." (Al-Baqarah: 144), which is the Ka‘bah.
